title: Pollo en Mole Chicken Mole Sauce

seo.title: Authentic Chicken Mole Recipe (Pollo en Mole Sauce)
seo.metaDescription: Learn to make authentic Chicken Mole (Pollo en Mole) with our easy, step-by-step recipe. Rich, complex, and flavorful Mexican chicken mole sauce from scratch. Get the best recipe!
seo.h1: Authentic Chicken Mole Recipe (Pollo en Mole)
h2Headings: What is Chicken Mole (Pollo en Mole)?
Step-by-Step Guide to Making Chicken Mole
Pro Tips for the Best Chicken Mole Sauce
Serving Suggestions for Pollo en Mole
Variations and Substitutions for Your Mole
seo.imageAlt: A plate of authentic Mexican Chicken Mole (Pollo en Mole) with rich, dark mole sauce, chicken, and sesame seeds.
seo.imageCaption: Savor the rich, complex flavors of homemade Chicken Mole, a traditional Mexican dish perfect for any occasion.

description: Pollo en Mole is a deeply flavorful and iconic Mexican dish featuring tender chicken simmered in a rich, complex mole sauce. This traditional recipe balances sweet, savory, and spicy notes, creating an unforgettable culinary experience. Perfect for celebrations or a comforting family meal, our easy version brings the authentic taste of Mexico to your kitchen.

instructions.title: Prepare the Chicken
instructions.text: In a large pot, combine chicken pieces, half of the chopped onion, 2 cloves of garlic, and enough chicken broth to cover. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat and simmer for 25-30 minutes until chicken is cooked through. Remove chicken, reserving 2 cups of the cooking broth. Set chicken aside.

instructions.title: Toast Aromatics
instructions.text: In a dry skillet over medium heat, toast the almonds and 1 tablespoon of sesame seeds until lightly golden and fragrant, about 2-3 minutes. Be careful not to burn them. Set aside.

instructions.title: Sauté Vegetables
instructions.text: In the same skillet, add 1 tablespoon of vegetable oil. Sauté the remaining chopped onion, 1 clove of garlic, and quartered tomatoes until softened, about 5-7 minutes.

instructions.title: Blend Mole Base
instructions.text: Transfer the sautéed vegetables, toasted almonds, and 1 tablespoon of toasted sesame seeds to a blender. Add 1 cup of the reserved chicken broth. Blend until very smooth.

instructions.title: Cook the Mole Sauce
instructions.text: In a large saucepan or pot, heat the remaining 1 tablespoon of vegetable oil over medium heat. Add the mole paste and stir constantly for 2-3 minutes to 'fry' it, releasing its flavors. Carefully pour in the blended mixture and the remaining 1 cup of reserved chicken broth. Stir well to combine.

instructions.title: Simmer and Season
instructions.text: Bring the sauce to a gentle simmer, stirring frequently to prevent sticking. Add the sugar and season with salt to taste. Continue to simmer for 15-20 minutes, allowing the sauce to thicken and flavors to meld.

instructions.title: Combine Chicken and Mole
instructions.text: Gently add the cooked chicken pieces to the mole sauce. Ensure the chicken is fully coated. Simmer for another 5-10 minutes, allowing the chicken to absorb the rich flavors of the mole.

instructions.title: Serve
instructions.text: Serve the Pollo en Mole hot, garnished with extra sesame seeds. It's traditionally served with warm corn tortillas and Mexican rice.

tips: To deepen the flavor of your mole, lightly toast the mole paste in a dry pan for a minute or two before adding liquids. This 'fries' the spices and releases their full aroma.

If you cant find a good quality mole paste, you can start with a mole base or even a mole powder, but adjust the spices and sweetness to your preference. A touch of dark chocolate (Mexican chocolate is ideal) can add richness.
Mole sauce freezes beautifully. Make a larger batch and freeze portions for quick future meals. Its even better the next day as the flavors continue to meld.
article:
Embark on a culinary journey to the heart of Mexico with Pollo en Mole, a dish that transcends mere sustenance to become a celebration of heritage, flavor, and technique. This iconic Mexican chicken recipe, often simply known as Chicken Mole, is a symphony of complex flavors – a rich, velvety sauce coating tender chicken, promising an unforgettable dining experience. While the prospect of making mole from scratch might seem daunting, the reward of a homemade, authentic Chicken Mole is immeasurable. Join us as we demystify this culinary masterpiece, guiding you through its origins, ingredients, and preparation, ensuring you can bring the magic of Mexican mole to your own kitchen.

## What is Chicken Mole (Pollo en Mole)?

At its core, "mole" (pronounced MOH-lay) is a generic term for a variety of complex sauces in Mexican cuisine, often characterized by a long list of ingredients and a meticulous preparation process. Among these, Mole Poblano, originating from Puebla, is arguably the most famous, and it's this rich, dark sauce that typically forms the basis of Pollo en Mole. This legendary sauce is a fascinating blend of chiles, spices, nuts, seeds, fruits, and a touch of chocolate, creating a flavor profile that is simultaneously savory, subtly sweet, earthy, and mildly spicy.

The history of mole is steeped in legend, with stories often placing its creation in colonial-era convents, where nuns are said to have combined various pantry staples to create a dish fit for visiting dignitaries. Regardless of its exact origins, mole has become a cornerstone of Mexican gastronomy, particularly revered for special occasions and holidays. When paired with succulent chicken, the dish transforms into Pollo en Mole, a comforting and deeply satisfying meal that embodies the soul of Mexican cooking. The sauce itself is incredibly versatile, but its affinity for chicken has made Chicken Mole a beloved classic across Mexico and beyond.

## Ingredients for Authentic Chicken Mole

Creating an authentic Chicken Mole sauce is an act of love, requiring a diverse array of ingredients that contribute to its distinctive depth. While exact measurements can vary, the following components are essential:

*   **The Chiles (The Heart of Mole):** A trio of dried chiles forms the backbone: Ancho (for fruity sweetness and mild heat), Mulato (for smoky, chocolatey notes), and Pasilla (for earthy, raisin-like flavors). Some recipes also include a small amount of Chipotle for a smoky kick.
*   **Aromatics:** White onion and garlic provide fundamental savory notes.
*   **Spices:** A carefully balanced blend typically includes cumin, coriander, cloves, cinnamon stick, black peppercorns, and sometimes a hint of star anise or allspice.
*   **Nuts and Seeds:** Toasted almonds, pumpkin seeds (pepitas), and sesame seeds contribute richness, texture, and a nutty depth.
*   **Thickeners & Body:** Stale tortillas or bread (like bolillos or conchas) are often fried and blended in, providing body. Raisins or prunes add natural sweetness and a sticky texture.
*   **The Chocolate:** Mexican chocolate (often found in tablets like Ibarra or Abuelita) is traditional, but unsweetened dark chocolate (70-85% cocoa) can be used, with a little sugar added to taste. The chocolate doesn't make the mole sweet, but rather deepens its color and complexity.
*   **Broth:** High-quality chicken broth is crucial, both for cooking the chicken and thinning the mole sauce to the desired consistency.
*   **Chicken:** A whole chicken cut into pieces, or bone-in, skin-on chicken thighs and drumsticks, are ideal for simmering and absorbing the rich flavors.
*   **Fats and Seasoning:** Lard or a neutral oil for frying, and salt and sugar to balance the flavors.

## Step-by-Step Guide to Making Chicken Mole

While the ingredient list is long, breaking down the process makes preparing Chicken Mole manageable and enjoyable:

1.  **Prepare the Chiles:** Carefully remove stems and seeds from the dried chiles. Briefly toast them in a dry skillet until fragrant (be careful not to burn them, or they'll turn bitter). Rehydrate the toasted chiles by soaking them in hot water for at least 30 minutes until soft.
2.  **Toast Nuts, Seeds, and Spices:** In a dry skillet, toast the almonds, pumpkin seeds, and sesame seeds separately until golden and fragrant. Set aside. Lightly toast the whole spices (cumin seeds, peppercorns, cinnamon stick, etc.) until aromatic, then grind them into a powder.
3.  **Sauté Aromatics and Thickeners:** In a pan with a little oil or lard, sauté sliced onion and garlic until softened. Add the stale tortillas or bread and fry until lightly golden.
4.  **Blend the Paste:** Drain the rehydrated chiles (reserving some soaking liquid). Combine the chiles, toasted nuts, seeds, ground spices, sautéed aromatics, fried tortillas/bread, and raisins/prunes in a blender. Add a cup or two of chicken broth and blend until a very smooth paste forms. You may need to do this in batches, adding more broth as needed to achieve a thick, pourable consistency. For an extra smooth sauce, pass the blended paste through a fine-mesh sieve.
5.  **Fry the Mole Paste:** This is a crucial step for developing deep flavor. Heat a generous amount of lard or oil in a large, heavy-bottomed pot or Dutch oven over medium heat. Carefully add the mole paste (it will splatter!) and fry, stirring constantly, for at least 20-30 minutes. The paste will darken and thicken, and its flavors will intensify.
6.  **Add Chocolate and Simmer:** Stir in the Mexican chocolate (or unsweetened dark chocolate) until completely melted and incorporated. Gradually add chicken broth, stirring continuously, until the sauce reaches your desired consistency – it should be thick enough to coat the back of a spoon but still pourable. Bring to a gentle simmer.
7.  **Cook the Chicken:** While the mole simmers, poach or braise your chicken pieces in seasoned chicken broth until tender and cooked through. Once cooked, transfer the chicken to the simmering mole sauce.
8.  **Combine and Adjust:** Let the chicken simmer gently in the mole sauce for at least 30 minutes to an hour, allowing the flavors to meld. Taste and adjust seasoning with salt and a pinch of sugar if needed to balance the flavors. The final Chicken Mole should be rich, balanced, and utterly delicious.

## Pro Tips for the Best Chicken Mole Sauce

*   **Patience is a Virtue:** Mole is not a dish to rush. The slow toasting of ingredients, the thorough blending, and especially the long simmering of the sauce are key to its depth and complexity.
*   **Don't Skip Frying the Paste:** Frying the mole paste after blending is non-negotiable. This step toasts the raw flavors of the chiles and other ingredients, deepening their character and ensuring a richer, more nuanced sauce.
*   **Taste, Taste, Taste:** As you cook, continuously taste your mole sauce. This allows you to adjust the balance of sweet, savory, and spicy elements. A pinch of sugar can temper bitterness, while a touch more salt can bring out other flavors.
*   **Quality Ingredients Matter:** Since mole has so many components, using fresh, high-quality ingredients, particularly the dried chiles and chocolate, will make a noticeable difference in the final product.
*   **Make Ahead:** Mole often tastes even better the next day, as the flavors have more time to meld. It also freezes beautifully, making it an excellent candidate for meal prep.

## Serving Suggestions for Pollo en Mole

Once your glorious Pollo en Mole is ready, presenting it traditionally enhances the experience:

*   **Classic Pairing:** Serve your Chicken Mole generously over a bed of fluffy white rice, which provides a neutral canvas for the sauce's rich flavors.
*   **Warm Tortillas:** Accompany with a stack of warm corn tortillas (or flour tortillas, if preferred) for scooping up every last drop of the exquisite sauce.
*   **Garnishes:** A sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds adds a lovely crunch and visual appeal. Finely chopped raw onion and cilantro can also offer a fresh counterpoint.
*   **Simple Sides:** A side of refried beans or a light, refreshing salad can complement the richness of the mole without overpowering it.
*   **Drink Pairings:** A tall glass of horchata, a refreshing agua fresca, or a crisp Mexican lager can perfectly balance the robust flavors of the Chicken Mole.

## Variations and Substitutions for Your Mole

While the classic Chicken Mole is a masterpiece, feel free to explore variations to suit your taste or dietary needs:

*   **Chile Combinations:** Experiment with different dried chiles. A single pasilla de Oaxaca can add a stronger smoky note, or a small amount of árbol chile can increase the heat.
*   **Nut and Seed Alternatives:** Walnuts, peanuts, or pecans can be used in place of or in addition to almonds and pepitas, each lending a slightly different flavor profile.
*   **Chocolate Adjustments:** Adjust the amount of chocolate to your preference. For a less pronounced chocolate flavor, use less or opt for a higher cocoa percentage.
*   **Protein Swaps:** While Pollo en Mole is traditional, mole sauce is incredibly versatile. It pairs beautifully with turkey (especially for Thanksgiving leftovers!), pork, duck, or even vegetables like roasted sweet potatoes or cauliflower for a vegetarian option.
*   **Time-Saving Shortcuts:** If time is a major constraint, you can find high-quality pre-made mole pastes in specialty Mexican markets. While not quite the same as homemade, they offer a delicious shortcut.
*   **Other Moles:** Remember that "mole" is a family of sauces. Explore other types like Mole Verde (green mole, often made with tomatillos and fresh herbs) or Mole Amarillo (yellow mole), which offer entirely different flavor experiences.

Creating Chicken Mole from scratch is a rewarding culinary adventure that connects you to centuries of Mexican tradition. It's a dish that speaks of celebration, family, and the profound joy of sharing good food. With this guide, you're now equipped to bring the magic of Pollo en Mole into your home, transforming humble chicken into a dish that will impress and delight. So, gather your ingredients, embrace the process, and prepare to savor one of Mexico's most cherished culinary treasures.
